coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots for an inner filter effect-based cyanide sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Chuanxia
Zhao, Dan
Hu, Tao
Sun, Jian
Yang, Xiurong
coredata.dc:description: In this work, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,S-GQDs), exhibiting bright blue fluorescence with an excellent quantum yield of 67%, were facilely prepared by one-pot pyrolysis of citric acid (carbon source) and cysteine (N and S sources). Compared to conventional GQDs, the doping of nitrogen/sulfur had significantly altered and uniformed the surface state, and the as-obtained N,S-GQDs displayed an excitation-independent emission behavior, where the fluorescence decay curve was nearly a single exponential. On the basis of the well-known inner filter effect of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) and cyanide (CN−)-induced etching of AgNPs, the fluorescence of N,S-GQDs could be quenched by AgNPs, and the nonfluorescence state of the as-prepared N,S-GQD-AgNP ensemble would be switched on in the presence of CN−. Meanwhile, the addition of N,S-GQDs has almost ignorable effect on the absorption spectrum of the AgNP solution, however, the subsequent introduction of CN− would significantly decrease the absorbance value owing to the aforementioned etching behavior of CN−. Therefore, a N,S-GQD-based fluorescent and AgNP-related colorimetric dual-mode analytical system for efficacious determination of CN− has been rationally designed and successfully developed for the first time. Furthermore, the detection limits were found to be 0.52μM and 0.78μM for fluorescent and colorimetric sensors individually under the optimal experiment conditions. The proposed N,S-GQD-AgNP-based assay can be successfully utilized to the quantitative determination of CN− in spiked tap water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots
Silver nanoparticles
Inner filter effect
Cyanide
Dual-mode

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Establishment of the colorimetric and fluorescence sensing system for CN−
Optimization of the experimental conditions for CN− detection
Colorimetric and fluorescence detection of CN−
Detection of CN− in tap water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the sensing process for CN− based on an AgNP-GQD probe.

(A) TEM images of the obtained N,S-GQDs. Inset: size distribution. (B) XRD patterns of the N,S-GQDs and N-GQDs. (C) AFM image of the N,S-GQDs. (D) Height profile along the line in (C) and height distribution of the N,S-GQDs.
(A) XPS survey spectra of the N,S-GQDs. (B–D) High-resolution C1s, N1s and S2p peaks of the N,S-GQDs. (E) FTIR spectra of the citric acid, L-Cysteine and N,S-GQDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the obtained N,S-GQDs. Inset: Photographs of the concentrated N,S-GQDs under illumination of white light (a), and the diluted solution under illumination of white light (b) and UV (365nm) light (c). (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of the N,S-GQDs with excitation of different wavelength. (C) Fluorescence intensity of N,S-GQDs in 6 months. (D) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of N,S-GQDs. Inset: Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the N,S-GQDs in solutions of different pH (1.0–14.0 in 1.0 increments).

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of N,S-GQDs, N,S-GQDs+KCN,N,S-GQDs+AgNPs and N,S-GQDs+AgNPs+KCN systems. (B) Fluorescence spectra of N,S-GQDs, N,S-GQDs+KCN,N,S-GQDs+AgNPs and N,S-GQDs+AgNPs+KCN systems.

(A) Fluorescence excitation (a) and emission (b) spectra of the N,S-GQDs, and UV–vis absorption spectrum (c) of the as-prepared AgNPs. (B) Time-resolved fluorescence decays of N,S-GQDs (black curve), N,S-GQDs in the presence of AgNPs (red curve) and in the presence of AgNPs and CN− (blue curve). (C) Test about the centrifugation by getting rid of AgNPs on the variation of fluorescence emission intensity of the N,S-GQDs. (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of N,S-GQDs, AgNPs, as well as the experimental and the theoretical profile of N,S-GQDs+AgNPs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) The F/F0 against volumes of AgNPs in the presence of 400μM CN−. (B) Bars represent the fluorescence responses at 415nm of GQD-AgNP system to buffer pH in the absence and presence of CN−. Inset: F/F0 as a function of pH values. (C) The F/F0 against time in the presence of different concentrations of CN−. (D) The A398 against time.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of the N,S-GQD-AgNP system with various concentrations of CN−. (B) Relationship between the ΔA398 and the concentration of CN−. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of N,S-GQD-AgNP system in the presence of different concentrations of CN−. (D) The fluorescence enhancement efficiency as a function of CN− concentration. (E) Photographs of the visual color change corresponding to (A) (Arrow direction: 0, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 400, 500μM).

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots for an inner filter effect-based cyanide sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, Jilin 130022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, 230026, Anhui, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Science and Technology of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,S-GQDs), exhibiting bright blue fluorescence with an excellent quantum yield of 67%, were facilely prepared by one-pot pyrolysis of citric acid (carbon source) and cysteine (N and S sources). Compared to conventional GQDs, the doping of nitrogen/sulfur had significantly altered and uniformed the surface state, and the as-obtained N,S-GQDs displayed an excitation-independent emission behavior, where the fluorescence decay curve was nearly a single exponential. On the basis of the well-known inner filter effect of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) and cyanide (CN−)-induced etching of AgNPs, the fluorescence of N,S-GQDs could be quenched by AgNPs, and the nonfluorescence state of the as-prepared N,S-GQD-AgNP ensemble would be switched on in the presence of CN−. Meanwhile, the addition of N,S-GQDs has almost ignorable effect on the absorption spectrum of the AgNP solution, however, the subsequent introduction of CN− would significantly decrease the absorbance value owing to the aforementioned etching behavior of CN−. Therefore, a N,S-GQD-based fluorescent and AgNP-related colorimetric dual-mode analytical system for efficacious determination of CN− has been rationally designed and successfully developed for the first time. Furthermore, the detection limits were found to be 0.52μM and 0.78μM for fluorescent and colorimetric sensors individually under the optimal experiment conditions. The proposed N,S-GQD-AgNP-based assay can be successfully utilized to the quantitative determination of CN− in spiked tap water samples.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), the graphene sheets with lateral dimensions less than 100nm [1], have fuelled intensive research interest recently owing to their outstanding optical properties, low toxicity, high stability, good biocompatibility and robust organic inertness derived from the quantum confinement and edge effects [2–4].Therefore, recent advances have been made in the development of effective strategies to prepare GQDs. Generally, the approaches for synthesizing GQDs can be classified into two main groups: top-down [5–7] and bottom-up methods [8,9]. The top-down methods, which primarily refer to cutting of larger graphene sheets into nanosized ones by using physical or chemical approaches, are usually not very satisfactory due to complex process, severe conditions, expensive starting materials and low product yield [10,11]. Alternatively, bottom-up approaches have been devised to achieve fluorescent GQDs. Especially intriguing, doping GQDs with heteroatoms (such as N, B, Si and S) is easier in bottom-up routes by carefully selecting the diversified precursors, which would effectively tune the intrinsic properties of GQDs, including optical characteristics, surface and local chemical features[12–16]. N,S-CDs with high fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) have been synthesized through hydrothermal treatment [17], but complex and time-consuming pretreatment processes were involved. A simple microwave-assisted method was also developed to prepare graphitic-like N,S-CDs with blue fluorescence [18]. It owns a less-than-ideal FLQY and extra urea was indispensable in the route. Therefore, efficient ways to get N,S-GQDs with both improved FLQY and simple process are still highly desired, and pyrolysis treatment [19] might be a good option. Additionally, cysteine, one of the simplest and most common N,S-containing biological molecules, would be an ideal candidate for doping GQDs with N and S atoms.

The excellent fluorescence properties of GQDs that are similar to those of semiconductor QDs make them promising in chemosensing and biosensing. Conventionally, target analytes sensing by GQDs was always based on energy and/or charge transfer from GQD architectures to the targets, which is often marked by fluorescence quenching of the GQDs [20–23]. However, the “turn off” sensing principle usually suffers from relatively low sensitivity and is susceptible to external factors, leading to false positives. In contrast, the “turn on” fluorescence sensors help to facilitate detection results more reliable and have been extensively developed, recently. Thereinto, incorporating GQDs into the design of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner-filter effects (IFE) systems to establish elaborated “turn on” sensors is one of the thought-provoking topics [24,25].

Cyanide (CN−) is known as a highly toxic and dangerous anion which affects the ability of cells to utilize oxygen [26]. Hence, there is considerable interest in finding readily available, simple-to-use and inexpensive methods to detect CN−. By taking advantages of the capability of CN− to etch noble metal nanoparticles (such as AuNPs and AgNPs) [27], simple colorimetric sensors for CN− have been already reported. Furthermore, it has reported that based on IFE of AuNPs or AgNPs, fluorescence chemosensors with improved sensitivity and selectivity could be easily achieved by using organic fluorophore as fluorometric reporter [28]. On one hands, previously reported CN− sensors were mainly focused on the single optical readout. To make the experimental results more credible and versatile, method with multiple outputs to complement each other is needed. On the other hands, GQDs are more promising fluorophores in fabrication of IFE-based sensors due to their superior optical properties [29], and no GQDs participant sensor for CN− has been reported.

Herein, we prepared the N,S-GQDs with high FLQY by a simple one-pot pyrolysis method, using citric acid (CA) and cysteine as starting materials. The use of cysteine in synthetic process could uniform the surface states and increase the FLQY of GQDs efficiently through doping GQDs with heteroatoms. Subsequently, the AgNP-quenched fluorescence of GQDs and the quenching theory as well as the dual-mode sensor construction for CN− therewith were studied comprehensively. Compared with the existing organic fluorophore-based CN− detection strategies [28,30], intriguing features of this sensing system were that the unique fluorescence properties of GQDs and the dual-readout modes can ensure a more convincing and ideal signal output. The special interaction between CN− and AgNPs rendered this IFE-based sensor facile, economic as well as highly selective.

Citric acid was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). L-Cysteine and L-Alanine were obtained from Shanghai Sangon Biotech. Co., Ltd. (China). Sodium sulfide (Na2S) was procured from Chengdu Cracia Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (China). NaF, NaCl, NaClO4, NaBr, NaBrO3, NaI, NaNO2, NaNO3, Na2SO4, Na2SO3, NaSCN, Na3PO4, Na2CO3, NaAc and EDTA were bought from Beijing Chemical Reagent Co. (China). All of these reagents were analytical grade and used as received. The water used throughout the experiments was ultrapure water purified by a Milli-Q water system (resistivity 18.25 MΩ cm, Millipore, USA).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of GQDs and AgNPs were obtained using a Tecnai G2 F20 (FEI) transmission electron microscope operated at 200kV. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) image was performed on a NanoScope Multimode AFM (Veeco, USA) using the tapping mode AFM. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed by using an ESCALAB MK II spectrometer (VG Scientific) with Al Kα radiation as the X-ray source. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) study was carried out using Bruker D8 Advance (Bruker AXS, Germany) with a graphite monochromator using Cu Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) data were acquired with a VERTEX 70 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by Cary 50 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Varian). Fluorescence measurements were carried out using a F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi). The time-resolved fluorescence decay was performed on an Edinburgh FLSP-920 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh, UK) with a 375nm LED as the excitation source.

N,S-GQDs were prepared by pyrolysis of CA and L-Cysteine at 240°C. Briefly, 1.0g CA was initially mixed with 0.30g L-Cysteine. Then the mixture was put into a 5mL beaker and heated to 240°C using a heating mantle. About 2min later, the mixture was liquated. Subsequently, the color of the liquid was changed from colorless to pale yellow and finally orange in 10min. Then, the liquid was dissolved into Milli-Q ultrapure water. The resultant orange N,S-GQD solution was stored in the dark at room temperature.
As a control, undoped-GQDs were synthesized by using CA as the carbon source, and N-GQDs were synthesized by using CA as the carbon source and L-Alanine as the nitrogen source, respectively.

AgNPs were synthesized by using ascorbic acid as reductant and citrate as stabilizer [31]. All glasswares used in the preparation were soaked in aqua regia (HCl:HNO3 =3:1) and rinsed thoroughly with ultrapure water before use. Typically, an 8.0mL aqueous solution containing ascorbic acid (0.6mM) and trisodium citrate (3.0mM) was adjusted to pH 10.0 by 0.1M NaOH solution. Then 0.08mL of 100mM AgNO3 was added under a stirring speed of 900rpm in a 30°C water bath. The reaction solution was observed to change from colorless to yellow, and 15min later, no further change in color took place, indicating the reaction was complete. The obtained AgNPs were aged at 100°C for 2h to become more spherical-like in shape. The resultant AgNP solution was cooled to room temperature and stored in the dark at 4°C.

A freshly prepared mixture solution containing 10μL of 41.7μg/mLN,S-GQDs and 100μL of AgNPs in NaOH-NaHCO3 buffer (10mM, pH 10.0) was used for CN− detection, into which KCN with different concentrations were added. The mixture solution was equilibrated for 5min at ambient temperature, and then used for spectral measurements. Each sample was repeated three times.

As previously reported, after the moderate pyrolysis procedure, citric acid can be partially carbonized to form GQDs containing abundant small sp2 clusters that isolated within the whole graphite structure [19]. In this work, to tune the optical characteristics, surface state and local chemical features, L-Cysteine was introduced to dope GQDs with N and S atoms by a simple one-pot pyrolysis treatment (Scheme S1, Electronic Supplementary information (ESI)).

As shown in the TEM image (Fig. 1 A), the diameters of N,S-GQDs were mainly distributed in the narrow range of 1.25–2.75nm with an average diameter of 2.21nm. Though no high resolution TEM images were obtained in this work to state the lattice spacing of graphene, the XRD patterns of GQDs showed a broad (002) peak centered at around 25.5° (Fig. 1B and Fig. S1, ESI), which preliminarily confirmed the graphene-like structure of the as-prepared N,S-GQDs as well as the N-GQDs and undoped-GQDs [9,19]. The topographic heights of the N,S-GQD sheet were mainly between 1.0-2.0nm based on the typical AFM image (C and D, Fig. 1), corresponding to ca. 2–6 graphene layers [5,9].

In order to analyze the composition of GQDs, XPS and FTIR spectra were recorded. The XPS survey spectrum of the N,S-GQDs showed a predominant graphitic C1S peak at ca. 285eV, an O1S peak at ca. 532eV, a S2P peak at ca. 163eV, and a N1S peak at ca. 400eV (Fig. 2 A). The presence of sulfur and nitrogen elements confirmed the successful doping of GQDs with cysteine by a one-pot pyrolysis reaction. The elemental analysis of N,S-GQDs in Table S1 (ESI) also revealed that they were composed of nitrogen (3.31 at%), sulfur (3.42 at%), oxygen (19.63 at%) and carbon (73.64 at%). In comparison, N-GQDs were composed of nitrogen, oxygen and carbon, while undoped-GQDs contained only oxygen and carbon (Table S1, ESI). The high-resolution C1s spectra demonstrated three distinct peaks at 284.5, 286.0 and 288.5eV (Fig. 2B), which can be attributed to CC/CC in aromatic rings, CN (O), and COOH groups, respectively. The binding energy peak at 284.5eV confirmed the graphitic structure (sp2 CC) of the N,S-GQDs. The high-resolution spectrum of N1S (Fig. 2C) revealed the presence of both pyridinic type (400.3eV) and pyrrolic type (401.4eV) N atoms. And the presence of C–S–C units was revealed in the high-resolution spectrum of S2P (Fig. 2D) [17]. These results depicted that a great deal of O/N-containing groups including hydroxyl, carboxyl and amine functional groups were appeared on the surface of GQDs, which contributed to excellent water solubility of the as-prepared N,S-GQDs. The results of FTIR further confirmed the high occupation of oxygen-rich groups (Fig. 2E). The broad absorption bands at 3050–3500cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibrations of OH and NH (Fig. 2E and Fig. S2, ESI), indicating that there are lots of amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface, which endow the GQDs good hydrophilic properties [32]. The bands at 1708 and 1601cm−1 were attributed to the vibrational absorption bands of CO in COOH and CONH, respectively. The formation of CN and/or CS bonds was evidenced by absorption peaks at ca. 1195cm−1.

The as-prepared N,S-GQDs and undoped-GQDs showed similar orange color (inset, Fig. 3 A and Fig. S3A, ESI), while the N-GQDs displayed a green color (inset, Fig. S3B, ESI). A typical absorption peak at 346nm was observed for N,S-GQDs with a small band at 240nm (Fig. 3A). The peak at 240nm, consistent with the π→π* transition of CC, results in nearly no observed fluorescence emission [33]. Whereas the band-gap transition centered about 346nm, due to the trapping of excited state energy by the surface states, could lead to strong fluorescence emission [34]. In comparison, the undoped-GQDs showed a broad weak peak at ca.340nm, and N-GQDs displayed a strong absorption peak at ca. 334nm, both of which resulted in fluorescence emission. The full width at half maximum (FWHM) of absorption bands for N,S-GQDs and N-GQDs were 53nm and 64nm, respectively, implying a possible narrower size and surface state distribution of N,S-GQDs than N-GQDs [17,35].

Subsequent research on fluorescence spectra manifested that N,S-GQD aqueous solution can emit strong blue fluorescence under the illumination of UV (365nm) light (inset, Fig. 3A). The maximum excitation wavelength and emission wavelength were 346 and 415nm, respectively (Fig. 3A). Moreover, no perceptible shift of the emission wavelength was observed in the excitation wavelength range of 300–500nm (Fig. 3B), illustrating an excitation-independent emission of N,S-GQDs, which was completely different from most GQDs [16,36,37]. This excitation-independent emission behavior of N,S-GQDs in a much wider excitation wavelength range, compared to N-GQDs (280–400nm, Fig. S3B, ESI), implied that either the size or the surface state of the formed sp2 clusters in N,S-GQDs should be more uniform [38,39], resulting from the synergy effect of the doped N and S atoms. Conversely, undoped-GQDs showed an excitation-dependent emission (Fig. S3A, ESI), reflecting not only effect from sp2 clusters of different sizes but also the different emissive sites on each sp2 clusters [40]. The well-defined fluorescent band of the N,S-GQDs had a much narrower FWHM (60nm) than N-GQDs (71nm) and undoped-GQDs (89nm), which further confirmed the uniform localized surface state band structure contained in N,S-GQDs. Those results meant that doping of N and S elements might facilitate to uniform the surface state and size [18,41].

The quantum yield at 346nm excitation was estimated to be ∼67.0% for N,S-GQDs by using quinine sulfate as the standard, which was 8.59-fold higher than that of undoped-GQDs and three times higher than that of N-GQDs (Table S1, ESI). The highly uniform sizes and surface states of N,S-GQDs indicated defects mechanism was unsuitable to explain the high FLYQ. It was very likely originated from the increase in isolated sp2 conjugated carbon clusters promoted by heteroatom [42].

Apart from the bright fluorescence, the N,S-GQDs also possessed high stability, which offers another advantage for their future applications. Even after being kept for 6 months in air at room temperature, they still exhibited a transparent appearance and strong fluorescence (Fig. 3C). Additionally, the fluorescence intensities increased gradually by increasing pH values from 1.0 to 6.0 and remained strong and stable in the pH range of 6.0-12.0 (Fig. 3D). However, the emission spectra red shifted with the decrease of the pH values from 6.0 to 1.0, which was contrary to those of undoped-GQDs and N-GQDs (Fig. S4, ESI). The effect of the pH values could be understood in terms of the change in surface charge owing to protonation-deprotonation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of the colorimetric and fluorescence sensing system for CN−
ce:section.ce:para: As already reported, AgNPs can act as ideal fluorescence quenchers for many fluorophores due to their high extinction coefficients and broad energy absorption bandwidth in the visible range [28]. In this work, the fluorescence of N,S-GQDs could be readily quenched by AgNPs through IFE of AgNPs, which could be satisfied by overlap between emission spectrum as well as excitation spectrum of N,S-GQDs and absorption spectrum of AgNPs (Fig. 3), leading to a dramatic diminishment and red shift of the emission band of N,S-GQDs (a and c, Fig. 4 B). Correspondingly, the naked eye visible blue fluorescence of N,S-GQDs became shallow on exposure to a 365nm UV lamp, confirming the fluorescence quenching of the N,S-GQD solution (inset, Fig. 4B,). However, upon addition of KCN, AgNPs were efficiently oxidized by dissolved oxygen under alkaline conditions to form soluble and colorless complex, Ag(CN)2 − [28], which reduced the concentration of AgNPs and thus attenuated the IFE process, leading to the decrease of absorbance (curve d, Fig. 4A) and recovery of fluorescence (d, Fig. 4B). As a control, the color, fluorescence emission spectrum and absorption band of N,S-GQDs in the presence of CN− were almost identical to those of the individual N,S-GQDs (a and b, Fig. 4), which unambiguously illustrated that there was no obvious interaction between CN− and N,S-GQDs, and the enhanced emission should indirectly originate from the interaction of CN− with AgNPs. Based on this feature, excellent analytical performance for this dual-mode sensor for CN− with AgNP-related colorimetric and N,S-GQD-based fluorescent readout could be anticipated, as illustrated in Scheme 1 .

The quenching mechanism of N,S-GQDs by AgNPs was investigated in detail. The as-prepared yellow colored AgNPs were generally spherical in shape, highly monodisperse and fairly uniform with size of about 20nm (Fig. S5, ESI), and the characteristic surface plasmon absorption (SPR) band was observed at 398nm (c, Fig. 5 A). It was obvious that the absorption spectrum of AgNPs overlapped with both the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of GQDs to a considerable extent (Fig. 5), suggesting that IFE or FRET might be the main quenching pathway in this system [24,43]. To figure out the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence decays were examined. As depicted in Fig. 5B, N,S-GQDs, emission followed a single exponential decay, possessed a lifetime of about 10.15ns, and no decrease of lifetime (10.07ns) was observed in the presence of AgNPs. It ruled out the possibility of efficient FRET quenching of N,S-GQDs by AgNPs because non-radiative energy transfer is expected to substantially alter the exciton lifetime of the donor [44]. To further verify the mechanism of IFE, interactions such as electrostatic attraction and covalent binding between AgNPs and N,S-GQDs need to be excluded. On one hand, we centrifuged the mixed solution of AgNPs and N,S-GQDs, and then recorded the emission spectrum of the supernatant. After getting rid of the AgNPs, the emission intensity of the supernatant was nearly same with that of the individual N,S-GQDs, indicating no other interactions existed (Fig. 5C). On the other hand, the verdict that no complex formation or energy transfer was expected between N,S-GQDs and AgNPs could be supported by the unshifted SPR band of AgNPs in the presence of N,S-GQDs (Fig. 5D) [45], thereby proving definite IFE was the only possible dominated reason for the fluorescence quenching of N,S-GQDs induced by AgNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the experimental conditions for CN− detection
ce:section.ce:para: To improve the performance of the proposed sensor for CN−, several experimental conditions such as AgNP volume, pH value and incubation time were optimized. The fluorescence enhanced efficiency was defined as F/F0, where F and F0 corresponded to the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of CN−, respectively. Fig. S6 (ESI) signified that the fluorescence emission intensity of N,S-GQDs at 415nm declined proportionally to the volume of AgNPs in the range of 0–100μL, and then the decreasing tendency slowed down. That is to say, the fluorescence intensity was similarly sensitive to CN− (indeed the AgNPs) abstractly when AgNP volume was less than 100μL, whereas less AgNPs would induce a narrower linear range. Further increasing AgNPs would result in a low sensitivity. The alteration of F/F0 with increasing AgNPs volumes in the presence of 400μM CN− was also investigated. The results in Fig. 6 A shows that F/F0 increased first with the increasing volumes of AgNPs and then decreased, giving a maximum value at 100μL. Therefore, 100μL of AgNPs was selected for further detection experiments. As stated above, the fluorescence of the GQDs was strong and stable above pH 6.0, meanwhile, alkaline condition was preferred to neutral and acidic condition for etching of AgNPs by CN− [27]. Thus, the effect of pH in the 6.0-13.0 range was investigated and the results were revealed in Fig. 6B. F0 increased only slightly by increasing pH values, while F increased dramatically by increasing pH values from 6.0 to 8.0 and then reached a saturation level. For F/F0, it increased at first and then kept almost unchanged above pH 7.0, only beginning to decline slightly from pH 11, which indicated that the sensing system can keep stable and be applied for sensing of CN− in a wide pH range. In this study, pH 10.0 was selected for further investigations. It must be noted that the reaction between AgNPs and CN− was very fast. The fluorescence was fixed within 1min (Fig. 6C), and the absorbance achieved the stability in 3min (Fig. 6D) after the reagents were mixed. A reaction time of 5min was selected for better results.

To evaluate the selective recognition ability of GQD-AgNPs, several potentially interfering anions such as F−, Cl−, ClO4 −, Br−, BrO3 −, I−, NO3 −, NO2 −, CO3 −, Ac−, S2−, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, SCN−, PO4 3− and EDTA were tested separately. As demonstrated in Fig. S7A (ESI), no anion, except I− and S2−, can disturb the colorimetric detection of CN− significantly. The interference of I− and S2− arose from the aggregation of AgNPs induced by the two anions as I− and S2− can react strongly with AgNPs to form silver sulfide [46] and silver iodide [47], which attenuated the SPR band of AgNPs drastically (Inset, Fig. S7A, ESI). However, high concentrations of the two anions may not be encountered in most real samples such as drinking water and surface water samples. Furthermore, the presence of those anions has only a slight or negligible effect on the fluorescence response of the sensing system (Fig. S7B, ESI), demonstrating that the sensor had a highly selective response to CN−. The above observations enabled the selective and quantitative measurement of CN−.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorescence detection of CN−
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of the sensing system toward CN− was identified by measuring the absorbance changes of the SPR band at 398nm (ΔA398) and F/F0, respectively. Fig. 7 A elucidated that the increase of CN− concentration resulted in the systematic decrease of the absorbance at 398nm, accompanied by the visually gradual color fading (Fig. 7E). The ΔA398 increased linearly from 10 to 400μM CN− with a correlation coefficient R2 =0.997 (Fig. 7B). The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.78μM at S/N=3, and the limit of quality (LOQ) was figured out to be 2.5μM at S/N=10. As designed, the diminishment of IFE due to etching of AgNPs by CN− can also be evident by the fluorescence turn-on. The fluorescence intensity increased gradually with the increase of the CN− concentration from 10 to 500μM (Fig. 7C). And the fluorescence recovery correlated well to the CN− concentration range of 10–500μM with a correlation coefficient R2 =0.987 (Fig. 7D). The LOD and LOQ were individually calculated to be 0.52μM and 1.73μM, both of which were lower than the permitted value in drinking water fixed by the WHO (1.9μM) [48]. The sensitivity of the proposed sensor was comparable to or even better than most colorimetric and fluorescent CN− sensors reported [49,50], and the results were listed in Table S2 (ESI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CN− in tap water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further assess the feasibility of our proposed method, it was applied to quantitatively determine CN− in spiked tap water samples, which were obtained from our laboratory and filtered with 0.22μm membrane to remove impurities. Since the water samples were found to be free from CN−, known amounts of CN− were spiked into the samples. Satisfactory recoveries between 97.1% and 102.5% was obtained (Table S3, ESI), implying the accuracy and reliability of the present method for CN− determination in practical applications.

In summary, bright blue fluorescent N,S-GQDs, with a FLQY as high as 67%, were prepared by a simple one-pot pyrolysis method using citric acid as carbon source and cysteine as N and S sources. The as-prepared N,S-GQDs showed mainly 1.25-2.75nm in width, 1.0-2.0nm in height, and possessed excitation-independent emission and high stability. By employing the IFE of AgNPs, the fluorescence of N,S-GQDs could be quenched efficiently and the obtained GQD-AgNPs were then used as dual-mode colorimetric and fluorescent recovery sensors for CN− in water samples based on the etching of AgNPs by CN−. This work possesses some fantastic features over current literatures: (a) It proposed a simple method to get N,S-GQDs with high FLQY, good stability and intriguing fluorescent properties. (b) It gave an in-depth research on the quenching mechanism of N,S-GQDs. (c) The fluorimetric and colorimetric dual-readout increased the sensing diversity and accuracy. (d) The sensor could be applied in a wide pH range. The sensing method also featured simplicity, rapidity, cost-effectiveness, and high selectivity sensitivity. Furthermore, this strategy may pave a new avenue for extending the sensing applications of two dimension fluorescent nanomaterials (GQDs, MoS2, WS2, etc.).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21435005 and 21627808) and the Development Project of Science and Technology of Jilin Province (Grant No. 20150520011JH).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.11.010.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Preparation of graphene quantum dots/Ag nanoparticles nanocomposites for colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose

Graphene quantum dots derived from carbon fibers
Phonon-assisted electroluminescence from metallic carbon nanotubes and graphene
Bottom-up fabrication of photoluminescent graphene quantum dots with uniform morphology
Facile preparation and upconversion luminescence of graphene quantum dots
Transforming C-60 molecules into graphene quantum dots
Glutathione-functionalized graphene quantum dots as selective fluorescent probes for phosphate-containing metabolites
Highly luminescent S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots with broad visible absorption bands for visible light photocatalysts
Nitrogen-doped carbon nanotube arrays with high electrocatalytic activity for oxygen reduction
Nitrogen-doped graphene sheets grown by chemical vapor deposition: synthesis and influence of nitrogen impurities on carrier transport
Boron-doped graphene quantum dots for selective glucose sensing based on the abnormal aggregation-induced photoluminescence enhancement
Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive detection of Hg(II) ions
Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide prepared by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid
Facile synthesis of graphene quantum dots from 3D graphene and their application for Fe3+ sensing
Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of copper ions
Graphene quantum dots as fluorescence probes for turn-off sensing of melamine in the presence of Hg2+
Synthesis of highly luminescent graphitized carbon dots and the application in the Hg2+ detection
Graphene quantum dots assembled with metalloporphyrins for turn on sensing of hydrogen peroxide and glucose
AgNP-DNA@GQDs hybrid: new approach for sensitive detection of H2O2 and glucose via simultaneous AgNP etching and DNA cleavage
Silver nanoparticles as a cyanide colorimetric sensor in aqueous media
Turn-on fluorescent detection of cyanide based on the inner filter effect of silver nanoparticles
The use of luminescent quantum dots for optical sensing
Sensitive turn-on fluorescent detection of cyanide based on the dissolution of fluorophore functionalized gold nanoparticles
Size control over spherical silver nanoparticles by ascorbic acid reduction
A biocompatible fluorescent ink based on water-soluble luminescent carbon nanodots
Blue photoluminescence from chemically derived graphene oxide
Bandgap-like strong fluorescence in functionalized carbon nanoparticles
Surface plasmons on metal nanoparticles: the influence of shape and physical environment
Hybrid carbon source for producing nitrogen-doped polymer nanodots: one-pot hydrothermal synthesis, fluorescence enhancement and highly selective detection of Fe(III)
Highly photoluminescent amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots used for sensing copper ions
Shifting and non-shifting fluorescence emitted by carbon nanodots
On off on fluorescent carbon dot nanosensor for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on the inner filter effect
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
A facile, green, and solvent-free route to nitrogen-sulfur-codoped fluorescent carbon nanoparticles for cellular imaging

Detection of lead ions in water based on the surface energy transfer between gold nanoparticles and fluorescent dyes
Copper ion-selective fluorescent sensor based on the inner filter effect using a spiropyran derivative
Highly sensitive detection of caspase-3 activities via a nonconjugated gold nanoparticle-quantum dot pair mediated by an inner-filter effect
Use of fluorescent DNA-templated gold/silver nanoclusters for the detection of sulfide ions

The latest developments in quantifying cyanide and hydrogen cyanide
Recent progress in the development of fluorometric and colorimetric chemosensors for detection of cyanide ions

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chuanxia Chen obtained her BS degree in 2011 from Shandong Agricultural University, China. Currently, she is pursuing her Ph.D degree under the supervision of Prof. Xiurong Yang in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Her current research is the synthesis of functional nanomaterials and their chemosensors application.

Dan Zhao received her BS and MS degree from Central South University and Shenyang Institute of Applied Ecology, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2010 and 2013, respectively. Currently, she is pursuing her Ph.D degree under the supervision of Prof. Xiurong Yang in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Her research interests focus mainly on the development of colorimetric and fluorescence sensors for inorganic anions.

Tao Hu received his BS and MS degree from Anhui Normal University and University of Science and Technology of China in 2012 and 2015, respectively. Currently, he is pursuing her Ph.D degree under the supervision of Prof. Xiurong Yang in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. His research interests focus mainly on the development of colorimetric and fluorescence sensors for biomarkers in Alzheimer’s disease, biomolecule interactions.

Jian Sun is an associate professor of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. His research is mainly focused on the fabrication of biosensors.

Xiurong Yang is a professor of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Her current research includes study of biomolecules interaction, fabrication of biosensors, and synthesis of functional namomaterials.
